The approximate [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} length of a [λ](#lambda){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="parallel"} is the [cosine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine_and_cosine#:~:text=the%20ratio%20of%20the%20length%20of%20the%20adjacent%20leg%20to%20that%20of%20the%20hypotenuse) of its latitude in [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridians"}, [rad](#rad){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="radians"}, or [°](#deg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"}, depending on the input requirement of our cosine function: cos(\${parLat}\${conversionFactor}) = \${parLen}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: latinput
viewof latitude = Inputs.range([-.25, .25], {label: "Latitude", value: 0, step: .001})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: costype
viewof costype = Inputs.radio(["turns", "radians", "degrees"], {label: "Cosine input", value: "turns"})
```

# Dates and times {.hiddenheading #sec-dat}

Dec dates consist of a "year of era" ([yoe](#yoe){#yearofera .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year of era"}) and a "day of year" ([doy](#doy){#dayofyear .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day of year"}), whereas Dec times are composed of a "time of day" ([tod](#tod){#timeofday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="time of day"}) and a "time zone offset" ([tzo](#tzo){#timezoneoffset .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="time zone offset"}). In Zone [0]{.color0}, the current date is \${decYearP0hsl0}[+]{.mono}\${decDateP0hsl0} and the current time is \${decTimeP0hsl0}[-]{.mono}[0]{.color0}. Color labels ️make it easier to visually [parse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parsing#:~:text=a%20process%20of%20analyzing%20a%20string%20of%20symbols) the date and time that make up a Dec snap🫰: \${decYearP0hsl1}[+]{.mono}\${decDateP0hsl1}\${decTimeP0hsl1}[-]{.mono}[0]{.color0}.

# Millenium Year Day {.hiddenheading #sec-myd}

[Yoe](#yoe){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year of era"} color labels ️are based on "year of millenium" ([yom](#y){#yearofmillenium .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"}) values. At the start of every millennium, the [yom]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"} is [0]{.color0}. Halfway through a millenium, the [yom]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"} is [500]{.color5} [y](#y){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"}. [Doy](#doy){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day of year"} color labels ️are measured in [milliyears]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a year"} ([my](#my){#milliyear .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliyears"}). Every year starts on [Day 0]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="March 1"} ([0]{.color0} [my](#my){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliyears"}). The midyear point ([500]{.color5} [my](#my){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliyears"}) is noon ([500]{.color5} [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"}) on [Day 182]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="August 30"} in [common years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_year#:~:text=a%20calendar%20year%20with%20365%20days) and midnight ([0]{.color0} [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"}) on [Day 183]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="August 31"} in [leap years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leap_year#:~:text=a%20calendar%20year%20that%20contains%20an%20additional%20day).

# Day of dek (dod) {.hiddenheading #sec-dod}

Each [doy](#doy){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day of year"} also has two components. The first two digits of a three-digit [doy](#doy){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days of year"} represent a group of ten days called a [decaday](#doy){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="group of ten days"} ([dek](#dek){#decaday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="group of ten days"}). The last digit of a [doy]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days of year"} is the "day of dek" ([dod](#dod){#dayofdek .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day of dek"}). In Dec, [deks](#dek){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="groups of ten days"} are used instead of months and weeks. Likewise, Dec uses [dod](#dod){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days of dek"} in lieu of days of month ([dom](#dom){#dayofmonth .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days of month"}) and days of week ([dow](#dow){#dayofweek .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="days of week"}). In Zone [0]{.color0}, it is currently [Dek](#dek){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="group of ten days"} \${decDekP0hsl} and [Dod](#dod){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="Day of dek"} \${decDodP0hsl}.

# Zone equatorial meter (zem) {.hiddenheading #sec-zem}

:::{.column-page-right fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;font-size:.825rem;" id="zemmodulor" .lighthouse}
![](../asset/Modulor_measurements.svg){#zModu}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Modulor_measurements.svg#mw-jump-to-license)
:::

Apart from [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"}, Dec also measures distance using a unit called the **z**one **e**quatorial **m**eter (zem). The width of a Dec time zone at the Equator is approximately ten million ([~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"}) zem ([z](#z){#zoneequatorialmeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}). Similarly, the distance from the Equator to one of the Poles is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} [meters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metre#Definition:~:text=the%20base%20unit%20of%20length%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units). In other words, a [decimeridian]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a meridian"} ([dm](#dm){#decimeridian .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decimeridian"}) is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} long and a [quarter meridian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meridian_arc#Full_meridian_(polar_perimeter):~:text=The%20distance%20from%20the%20equator%20to%20the%20pole) is [~10^7^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately ten million"} meters long.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" id="zemhands" .hand}
{{< include _hands.qmd >}}
[[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Typing-colour_for-finger-positions.svg)]{.handlabel}
:::

# Length area volume {.hiddenheading #sec-lav}

You can approximate a [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} using your hands🤲. With your palms flat on a table in front of you and the tips of your thumbs👍touching, the maximum distance between the tips of your pinkies is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}. When you spread out the fingers on one hand✋or do the "[call me](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=the%20gesture%20is%20commonly%20understood%20to%20mimic%20the%20handset%20of%20a%20traditional%20landline%20telephone)", "[drink](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=placing%20the%20thumb%20to%20the%20mouth%20and%20motioning%20the%20little%20finger%20upward%20as%20if%20tipping%20up%20a%20bottle%27s%20bottom%20end)", or "[shaka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shaka_sign#:~:text=a%20gesture%20with%20friendly%20intent%20often%20associated%20with%20Hawaii%20and%20surf%20culture)"🤙gesture, your thumb and pinky tips are [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.5 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zems"} apart.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" #zemarms}
![](../asset/squareZem.svg){#zArms}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Extended_arm.jpg){#armlabel}
:::

To visualize a square [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} ([z&sup2;](#z2){#squarezem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"}), imagine four people standing in a circle, facing inward, each with their right hand placed on top of the elbow of the person to their right. Alternatively, two people can stand in front of each other and raise their arms💪, placing one hand on the elbow of the other person and the other hand on their own elbow.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" #zemlift}
![](../asset/Man_Lifting_Barbell_Cartoon.svg){#zLift}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Man_Lifting_Barbell_Cartoon.svg){#liftlabel}
:::

You can approximate a [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} yourself by sitting in a chair🪑or standing🧍with your knees and feet🦶1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, 4 [decimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a meter"}, or 16 inches apart, which is probably about the width of your hips or shoulders. The [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} will be between your shins, its top will be below your knees, and its bottom will be either above your ankles or feet, depending on your height.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;" id="zemcubic"}
{{< include _cubic.qmd >}}
[Dimensions.com](https://www.dimensions.com/element/sitting-male-side-1){#cubiclabel}
:::

# Typical seat height {.hiddenheading #sec-tsh}

According to [dimensions.com](https://www.dimensions.com), 115 [centizem](#cz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a zem"} ([cz](#cz){#centizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centizem"}) is the [typical seat height](https://www.dimensions.com/element/sitting-female-side-1#:~:text=Seat%20Height%20(Typical)%3A-,18%E2%80%9D%20%7C%2046%20cm,-Style%3A%20Casual) for both men and women age 25 to 45. A box📦that is the size of a cubic [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} ([z&sup3;](#z3){#cubiczem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic zem"}) would likely fit under a typical chair or in between the shins of two people sitting in front of each other with their knees and feet1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} apart and their legs🦵bent at 25 [centiturn]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundredth of a turn"} angles.

# Perpetually setting sun {.hiddenheading #sec-pss}

In [Slovak](https://sk.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zem)🇸🇰, [zem](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"} means Earth🌍. This is fitting because all Dec units are based on physical attributes of the Earth🌏. At the Equator, the Earth🌎rotates on its axis at a speed of [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}1.00224 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"}. If we could indefinitely maintain this speed while flying West in an airplane✈️towards the setting sun️, we would be able to perpetually fly [into the sunset](https://tvtropes.org/pmwiki/pmwiki.php/Main/RidingIntoTheSunset)🌅.

# Airplane cruising speed {.hiddenheading #sec-acs}

To travel fast enough for a perpetual sunset, the airplane ️would need to surpass the [speed of sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_sound#:~:text=the%20distance%20travelled%20per%20unit%20of%20time%20by%20a%20sound%20wave)🔊([sos](#sos){#speedofsound .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}), which at 15 [°](#deg){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="degrees"} [Celsius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Celsius#:~:text=the%20unit%20of%20temperature%20on%20the%20Celsius%20temperature%20scale) and 1 [standard atmosphere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standard_atmosphere_(unit)#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20pressure%20defined%20as%20101325%20Pa) is 0.735048 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} or Mach 1. [Mach numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mach_number) are relative to the [sos](#sos){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}, which varies greatly by air temperature and pressure. The cruising speed of a [Boeing 747](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boeing_747#:~:text=sweep%2C%20allowing%20a-,Mach%C2%A00.85,-%28490%C2%A0kn;%20900) is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.54 [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegars"} or Mach [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}0.85.

The highway🛣️speed of a car🚗is roughly tenfold slower than the cruising speed of an airplane️. If we are driving on a highway ️at a speed of 50 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"} and our exit is 1000 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zems"} away, we will have 20 [centimillidays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundred thousandths of a day"} ([cmd](#cmd){#centimilliday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centimilliday"}) until we have to exit the highway️. To ensure we do not miss our exit, we can periodically check a countdown of the remaining [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zems"}: \${zLeft}.

# Centimilliday (cmd) {.hiddenheading #sec-cmd}

Dec refers to [cmd](#cmd){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centimillidays"} as [beats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundred thousandths of a day"} ([b](#b){#beat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"}) because they are similar in duration to heart❤️beats or [musical beats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beat_(music)#:~:text=I-,n%20music%20and%20music%20theory%2C%20the%20beat%20is%20the%20basic%20unit%20of%20time,-%2C%20the). In Dec, 1 [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day"} = 100 [centiday]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a day"} ([cd](#cd){#centiday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centidays"}) = [10^5^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred thousand"} [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"} = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [microdays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millionths of a day"} ([µd](#ud){#microday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="microdays"}), 1 [mc](#mc){#millitaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millitaur"} = 100 [kilozem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of zem"} ([kz](#kz){#kilozem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of zems"}) = [10^5^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred thousand"} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zems"} = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [decizems]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a zem"} ([dz](#dz){#decizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizems"}) = [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [nanotaurs]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millionths of a taur"} ([nc](#nc){#nanotaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaurs"}), and therefore, 1 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegar"} = $\text{mc}\over\text d$ = $\text {kz}\over\text {cd}$ = $\text z\over\text b$ = $\text {dz}\over\text{µd}$ = $\text {nc}\over\text{µd}$. A [cd]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centiday"} is [96%]{.color960} of a quarter hour and a [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beat"} is [86.4%]{.color864} of a second.

# heart rate tempo {.hiddenheading #sec-hrt}

A [normal resting heart❤️rate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heart_rate#:~:text=heart%20rate%20is-,60–100%20bpm,-.%20An%20ultra%2Dtrained) is between 100 and 166.[6]{.vinculum} [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"} per [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"} ([bpm](#bpm){#beatpermilliday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}). The unofficial anthem of the Dec measurement system, "[Turn the beat around](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turn_the_Beat_Around#:~:text=a%20disco%20song%20written%20by%20Gerald%20Jackson%20and%20Peter%20Jackson%2C%20and%20performed%20by%20American%20actress%20and%20singer%20Vicki%20Sue%20Robinson%20in%201976)", has a [tempo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempo#:~:text=the%20speed%20or%20pace%20of%20a%20given%20composition) of 188.64 [bpm](#bpm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}, which corresponds to the _allegro_ [tempo marking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempo#Approximately_from_the_slowest_to_the_fastest). A Dec clock⏰ticks at a rate of 100 [bpm](#bpm){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats per milliday"}, $\text b^{-1}$, $1\over\text b$, 1 [inverse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplicative_inverse#:~:text=x%2C%20denoted%20by-,1/x%20or%20x%E2%88%921,-%2C%20is%20a%20number) beat, or 1 perbeat ([p](#per){#perbeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeat"}), which is [1.15[740]{.vinculum}]{.colorIob} times more frequent than a [Hertz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hertz#:~:text=one%20event%20(or%20cycle)).

# Frequency period wavelength {.hiddenheading #sec-fpw}

Dec uses [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeat"}, [b]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beats"}, and [z]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, often with metric prefixes, to measure the [frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency#:~:text=the%20number%20of%20occurrences%20of%20a%20repeating%20event%20per%20unit%20of%20time), [period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency#:~:text=the%20reciprocal%20of%20the%20frequency), and [wavelength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelength#:~:text=the%20distance%20over%20which%20the%20wave%27s%20shape%20repeats), respectively, of a sound or light wave. The equations below show how frequency, period, and wavelength are related to each other and to speed. The speed of light ([sol](#sol){#speedoflight .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of light"}) is roughly 647.551657 [kiloomegars]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of omegars"} ([kv](#kv){#kiloomegar .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kiloomegars"}), which is about 881 thousand times faster than the [sos](#sos){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="speed of sound"}.

$$\text{frequency} = \text{speed} \div \text{wavelength} = 1 \div \text{period}$$

$$\text{period} = \text{wavelength} \div \text{speed} = 1 \div \text{frequency}$$

$$\text{wavelength} = \text{speed} \times \text{period} = \text{speed} \div \text{frequency}$$

The frequency range of the  [visible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_spectrum#:~:text=the%20band%20of%20the%20electromagnetic%20spectrum%20that%20is%20visible%20to%20the%20human%20eye) spectrum of light is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}345.6 to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}914.4 [teraperbeats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="trillions of perbeats"} ([Tp](#Tp){#teraperbeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="teraperbeats"}). The range of sound frequencies which can be [audible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=the%20frequency%20range%20that%20can%20be%20heard%20by%20humans)👂for humans is [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[10.368](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=humans%20can%20hear%20sound%20as%20low%20as%2012%C2%A0Hz) to [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[24192](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hearing_range#:~:text=8%5D%20and-,as%20high%20as%2028%C2%A0kHz,-%2C%20though%20the%20threshold) [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}. The period and wavelength that correspond to the frequency chosen by the range input below are 1000 &div; \${iobs} [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} = \${parseFloat((1000 / iobs).toFixed(3))} [millibeats]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a beat"} ([mb](#mb){#millibeat .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millibeats"}) and 735.048 [mv](#mv){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliomegars"} &div; \${iobs} [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} = \${parseFloat((735.048 / iobs).toFixed(3))} [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: iobinput
//| class: freqcomponent
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
viewof iobs = Inputs.range([1, 9999], { step: 1,  value: 380, label: "Frequency" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: beatinput
//| class: freqcomponent
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
viewof beats = Inputs.range([1, 999], { step: 1,  value: 1, label: "Duration" })
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: iobplayer
//| class: freqcomponent
// https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds
Play((t) => Math.sin(iobs / .864 * t * 2 * Math.PI), beats * .864, iobs)
```

# Octave equals decastep {.hiddenheading #sec-oed}

In addition to [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}, the limits of human hearing can be expressed in musical [steps](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steps_and_skips#:~:text=the%20difference%20in%20pitch%20between%20two%20consecutive%20notes%20of%20a%20musical%20scale) ([s](#s){#step .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"}). The [range](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Range_(music)#:~:text=the%20distance%20from%20the%20lowest%20to%20the%20highest%20pitch%20it%20can%20play) of an 88-key piano is 23.76 to 3616.64 [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}, which is approximately [8]{.color8} to [80]{.color0} [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"}. The typical audible range for humans is [3]{.color3} [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"} to [103]{.color3} [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"}. In the equations below, δ is a decimal [decastep]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tens of musical steps"} value that can be used to calculate [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"}, and [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"}, and note ([n](#n){#note .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="musical notes"}) values measured in [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"}.

$$\delta = \log_2\!\left(\frac{\text{p}}{14.1275}\right) = \frac{\text{s}}{10}$$

$$\text{s} = 10 \times \delta = 10 \times \left\lfloor \delta \right\rfloor + \text{n}$$

$$\text{p} = 14.1275 \times 2^{\delta}$$

$$\text{n} = \text{s} - 10 \times \left\lfloor \delta \right\rfloor = 10 \times \left(\delta - \left\lfloor \delta \right\rfloor\right)
$$

# Color and sound {.hiddenheading #sec-cas}

Dec pairs each [n](#n){#note .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a musical note"} in an [octave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Octave#:~:text=an%20interval%20between%20two%20notes%2C%20one%20having%20twice%20the%20frequency%20of%20vibration%20of%20the%20other) with a color so that values can be labeled with both sounds and colors. In his [1704]{.color704} book entitled [Optiks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opticks#:~:text=a%20collection%20of%20three%20books%20by%20Isaac%20Newton)[^0], [Isaac Newton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Newton#:~:text=,an%20English%20polymath,-active%20as%20a) presented the first [color wheel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Color_wheel#:~:text=Newton's%20asymmetric%20color%20wheel%20based%20on%20musical%20intervals) and linked its colors to musical notes. On [[2025]{.color025}[+]{.mono}[080]{.colorD080}]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="May 20, 2025"}, I read [The Color of Sound](https://www.flutopedia.com/sound_color.htm) by [Clint Goss](https://www.clintgoss.com)[^1], which presents a method of connecting musical notes to colors via their frequencies.

The x-axis of the lollipop🍭plot below shows the rounded [p](#per){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="perbeats"} values of the ten Dec notes in Octave 4. The lollipops and their corresponding [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"} values are labeled with the ten Dec colors. The first digit of any [s](#s){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="steps"} value is its octave and the second digit is its [n](#n){#note .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="musical notes"}. Each octave provides a unique set of sound labels. Click and hold each lollipop to hear its sound label.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: lollipop
//| class: musicchart
// https://observablehq.com/@mcmire/tone-map
{
  const points = origs.map((orig, i) => ({
    origin: orig,
    x: orig,
    ratio: Fraction(1, 1),
    label: labels[i],
    color: colors[i],
    freq: hertz[i],
    alwaysShowLabel: true
  }));
  return renderGraph(points, {
    xAxis: {
      ticks: origs.map(Math.round)
    },
  });
}
```

# Système international d'unités {.hiddenheading #sec-sid}

As a scientist of European origin, I have a strong preference for the [International System of Units](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_System_of_Units#:~:text=the%20world%27s%20most%20widely%20used%20system%20of%20measurement) ([SI](#si){#internationalsystemofunits .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"}) over the United States🇺🇸([US](#us){#unitedstates .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"}) [customary measurement system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_and_US_customary_measurement_systems). Nevertheless, having grown up in the [US](#us){#unitedstates .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"}, I understand the animosity towards unfamiliar measurement units expressed by Grandpa Simpson in "A Star Is Burns", Season 6 Episode 18 of [the Simpsons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Simpsons#:~:text=an%20American%20animated%20sitcom).

![](/asset/metric.jpg)

# US customary units {.hiddenheading #sec-ucu}

Dec redefines [US](#us){#unitedstates .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units to facilitate conversion with [SI](#si){#internationalsystemofunits .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} and Dec units. The values in the first column of [unit conversion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conversion_of_units#:~:text=the%20conversion%20of%20the%20unit%20of%20measurement%20in%20which%20a%20quantity%20is%20expressed) table below are approximate [fold changes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fold_change#:~:text=measure%20describing%20how%20much%20a%20quantity%20changes%20between%20an%20original%20and%20a%20subsequent%20measurement) from original to redefined [US](#us){#unitedstates .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units. A fold change of [1]{.color0} means [0]{.color0} [change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relative_change#:~:text=compare%20two%20quantities%20while%20taking%20into%20account%20the%20%22sizes%22%20of%20the%20things%20being%20compared) is requires for alignment with the [SI](#si){#internationalsystemofunits .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} and Dec units shown in the second and third column, respectively.

## Unit conversion tables {.hiddenheading .unnumbered}

{{< include /dec/_lengthtable.qmd >}}

{{< include /dec/_speedtable.qmd >}}

{{< include /dec/_areatable.qmd >}}

{{< include /dec/_volumetable.qmd >}}

{{< include /dec/_masstable.qmd >}}

# Miles per hour (mph) {.hiddenheading #sec-mph}

Unlike Dec and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"}, the [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary measurement system does not use metric prefixes to scale units by [powers of ten](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_of_10#:~:text=any%20of%20the%20integer%20powers%20of%20the%20number%20ten). Redefined [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units serve as convenient reference points. [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary volume units have intuitive names and scale by [powers of two](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_of_two#:~:text=a%20number%20of%20the%20form%202n%20where%20n%20is%20an%20integer).

# Are hectare acre {.hiddenheading #sec-aha}

A square [kilozem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a thousand zems"} ([kz&sup2;](#kz2){#squarekilozem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square kilozem"}) is 1 [hexakilare]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="sixteen hundred ares"}, 16 [hectares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hectare#:~:text=a%20square%20with%20100%2Dmetre%20sides){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundreds of ares"}, 1600 [ares](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hectare#Are), 40 Dec [acres](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acre#:~:text=%20is-,a%20unit%20of%20land%20area,-used%20in%20the), 0.16 square [kilometers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilometre#:~:text=a%20unit%20of%20length%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of meters"} ([km&sup2;](#km2){#squarekilometer .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square kilometers"}), 0.0625 Dec [square miles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_mile#:~:text=US%20unit%20of%20measure%20for%20area), [10^6^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a million"} [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zems"}, or 1 megahexamilliare ([Mx](#Mx){#megahexamilliare .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="megahexamilliare"}). A [z&sup2;](#z2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square zem"} is 1 [hexamilliare]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="1.6 thousandths of an are"} ([x](#x){#hexamilliare .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexamilliare"}), 16 square [decimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="tenths of a meter"} ([dm&sup2;](#dm2){#squaredecimeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square decimeters"}), 1.[7]{.vinculum} Dec [square feet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_foot#:~:text=the%20area%20of%20a%20square%20with%20sides%20of%201%20foot)🦶 ([f&sup2;](#f2){#squarefoot .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square feet"}), or 256 Dec square inches ([i&sup2;](#i2){#squareinch .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square inch"}). A square [decazem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ten zem"} ([Dz&sup2;](#Dz2){#squaredecazem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square decazem"}) is 1 [hexadeciare]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="sixteen hundredths of an are"}, 16 [square meters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_metre#:~:text=the%20unit%20of%20area%20in%20the%20International%20System%20of%20Units) ([m&sup2;](#m2){#squaremeter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square meters"}), [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}19.75 Dec [square yards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_yard#:~:text=U.S.%20customary%20unit%20of%20area), or 100 [x](#x){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexamilliares"}.

# Drop wineglass keg {.hiddenheading #sec-dwk}

A cubic [decizem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a zone equatorial meter"} ([dz&sup3;](#dz3){#cubicdecizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic decizem"}) is 1 cubic [nanotaur]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a millionth of a taur"} ([nc&sup3;](#nc3){#cubicnanotaur .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic nanotaur"}), 1 Dec wineglass, 2 Dec ounces, 64 [milliliters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a liter"} ([mL](#mL){#milliliter .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="milliliters"}), 1000 Dec drops ([g](#g){#gutta .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="drops"}), or 1000 cubic [centizems]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a tenth of a zone equatorial meter"} ([cz&sup3;](#cz3){#cubiccentizem .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic centizem"}). A [dz&sup3;](#dz3){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic decizem"} of water🌊weighs $1\over7$ Dec pounds, 64 grams, or 1000 Dec grains ([g](#g){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grain"}). A Dec ounce ([u](#u){#ounce .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ounce"}) of water weighs $1\over14$ Dec pounds, 500 Dec [g](#g){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grains"}, or 32 grams. In Dec, the symbols [g](#g){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="grain"} and [u](#u){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="ounce"} can refer to either volume or mass. Mass divided by volume is density.

:::{.column-margin fig-align="center" style="text-align:center;"}
![](../asset/vitruvianMan.svg){#vitruvian}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Da_Vinci_Vitruve_Luc_Viatour_2.svg)
:::

A [z&sup3;](#z3){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="cubic zem"} is 1 keg. A keg of water🌊weighs 64 [kilograms]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of grams"}, 128 Dec pounds, or a 1000 Dec [kilograins]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousands of grains"} ([kg](#kg){#kilograin .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"}). If [Leonardo da Vinci](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci#:~:text=an%20Italian%20polymath%20of%20the%20High%20Renaissance)'s [Vitruvian Man](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vitruvian_Man#:~:text=a%20drawing%20by%20the%20Italian%20Renaissance%20artist%20and%20scientist%20Leonardo%20da%20Vinci) were 4 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zems"} tall, we could measure 1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} from his knees to his feet🦶or from his elbows💪to his fingertips. If he also weighed 1000 Dec [kg](#kg){#kilograin .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"}, his [body mass index](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Body_mass_index#:~:text=the%20body%20mass%20divided%20by%20the%20square%20of%20the%20body%20height) ([bmi](#bmi){#bodymassindex .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"}) would be 62.5 [kg](#kg){#kilograin .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"} per [x](#x){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexamilliares"} ($\text {kg}\over\text x$) or 25 kilograms per [m&sup2;](#m2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square meters"} ($\text {kilogram}\over\text m^2$).

# Body mass index (bmi) {.hiddenheading #sec-bmi}

A normal [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} ranges from 46.25 to 62.5 $\text {kg}\over\text x$ or 18.5 to 25 $\text {kg}\over\text m^2$. A person with a [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} above 75 $\text {kg}\over\text x$ or 30 $\text {kg}\over\text m^2$ can be classified as obese. A [bmi](#bmi){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="body mass index"} of \${kgrains} [kg](#kg){#kilograin .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="kilograins"} &div; \${zem2} [x](#x){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexamilliares"} = \${bmi} $\text {kg}\over\text x$ = \${kgrams} kilograms &div; \${meter2} [m&sup2;](#m2){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="square meters"} = ${bmim2} $\text {kilograms}\over\text m^2$ is considered \${bmiStr}.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: kginput
viewof kilograins = Inputs.range([0, 10000], {label: "Kilograins", value: 1000, step: 1})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| label: zinput
viewof zems = Inputs.range([0, 10], {label: "Zems", value: 4, step: 0.01})
```

# Centizem centimeter inch {.hiddenheading #sec-cci}

::: {.column-page-right fig-align="left" style="text-align:center;font-size:.825rem;"}
![](../asset/ruler1dz.svg){#ruler}
[Wikimedia](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ruler_illustration.svg)
:::

The longest length depicted in the image of a ruler📏above is 1 [dz](#dz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizem"}, 1 [nc](#nc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaur"}, 4 [centimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hundredths of a meter"}, or $8\over5$ Dec inches, and the shortest length is $1\over2$ [mz](#mz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millizems"}, $1\over5$ [millimeters]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="thousandths of a meter"}, $1\over125$ Dec inches, or $1\over127$ [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary inches. A [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary inch is $127\over2$ [mz](#mz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millizems"}, $127\over5$ millimeters, or $127\over125$ Dec inches. A Dec inch is $5\over2$ centimeters. A centimeter is $5\over2$ [cz](#cz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="centizems"}. A [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} is 4 decimeters. A decimeter is 4 Dec inches.

# Claude Boniface Collignon {.hiddenheading #sec-cbc}

In [1788]{.color788}, [Claude Boniface Collignon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Boniface_Collignon#:~:text=a%20French%20attorney%20who%20contributed%20to%20scientific%20and%20social%20reforms%20in%20the%20time%20of%20the%20French%20Revolution) proposed measuring length in [dz](#dz){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decizems"} or [nc](#nc){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanotaurs"} and tracking time in [deks](#dek){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="groups of ten days"}, [dd]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="decidays"}, [md](#md){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="millidays"}, [µd](#ud){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="microdays"}, and [nanodays]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="billionths of a day"} ([nd](#nd){#nanoday .tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="nanodays"})[^2]. On [[2025]{.color025}[+]{.mono}[039]{.colorD039}]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 9, 2025"}, while searching for units similar to [c]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} and [z]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, I noticed the definition of a [zem]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zone equatorial meter"}, 1 [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"} = [10^-8^]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="a hundred millionth"} [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"} = 40 centimeters, in a table of [ten possible length units](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos/history/sm/node12.html) from a [2004]{.color004} [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/physics/0412078) [article](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos/history/sm/sm.html) entitled "Why does the meter beat the second?"[^3].

# Summary {.unnumbered #tldr}

This article introduces the Dec measurement system and describes how Dec uses metric prefixes and the properties of the planet Earth🌍to define units based on turns for geographic coordinates, compass directions, dates, times, speeds, distances, areas, volumes, and weights. Each unit has a unique name, such as [λ](#lambda){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="parallel"}, [m](#m){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="meridian"}, [r](#r){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="rose"}, [y](#y){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="year"}, [d](#d){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="day"}, [b](#b){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="beat"}, [v](#v){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="omegar"}, [c](#c){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="taur"}, [z](#z){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="zem"}, or [x](#x){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="hexamilliare"}.

Dec attempts to bridge the gap, improve interoperability, and faciliate conversion between the [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary and [SI](#si){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="International System of Units"} measurement systems by redefining [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units. Redefinition of [US](#us){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="United States"} customary units makes inches [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[1.58%]{.color0158} shorter, miles [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[0.58%]{.colorMile} shorter, pints [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[5.67%]{.colorPint} larger, ounces [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[8.21%]{.colorFlOz} larger, and pounds [~]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="approximately"}[1.23%]{.colorMass} lighter.

Dec color labels can convey an impression of a value at a glance. [Xet](#xet){.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="Tenet"} sound labels allow us to estimate a value without even having to look at it. Both types of labels can help avoid confusion when [decimal separators](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decimal_separator#:~:text=In%20English%2Dspeaking%20countries%2C%20the%20decimal%20point%20is%20usually%20a%20small%20dot%20%28.%29%20placed%20either%20on%20the%20baseline) appear, disappear, or move due to a measurement unit change such as the addition, removal, or replacement of a metric prefix.

# Next {.unnumbered #next}

Now that you have had a taste of Dec, I hope that you are hungry for more! If so, dive🤿deeper into Dec [dates](/dec/date) and [times](/dec/time) before tackling Dec [snaps](/dec/snap) and [spans](/dec/span). My [filter](/quarto/filter), [include](/quarto/include), and [script](/quarto/script) articles discuss the [Quarto](/quarto) publishing system and how I display Dec [dates](/dec/date) in the [navigation bar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navigation_bar#:~:text=a%20section%20of%20a%20graphical%20user%20interface%20intended%20to%20aid%20visitors%20in%20accessing%20information), the [article list](/list), and [title blocks](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/title-blocks.html) of my website.

:::{#firstnav}
{{< include /asset/_decquanav.qmd >}}
:::

# Cite {.unnumbered #cite}

Of the several different [bibliography file formats](https://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#specifying-bibliographic-data) supported by [Quarto](https://quarto.org), I recommend storing [citations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citation#:~:text=a%20reference%20to%20a%20source) in [yaml](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML#:~:text=a%20human%2Dreadable%20data%20serialization%20language) files. With the `bibliography.yml` file shown below, you can cite this article and the [2021]{.color021} article entitled [[`chrono`]{.mono .under}-Compatible Low-Level Date Algorithms](https://howardhinnant.github.io/date_algorithms) in which [Howard Hinnant](https://howardhinnant.github.io) describes the algorithms that serve as the foundation of Dec dates [@hinnant2021].

```{.yml filename="bibliography.yml"}
---
references:
- id: laptev2025
  author:
    - family: Laptev
      given: Martin
  issued:
    literal: {{< meta date >}}
  title: Dec
  type: article
  URL: https://maptv.github.io/dec
- id: hinnant2021
  author:
    - family: Hinnant
      given: Howard
  issued:
    literal: 2021+185
  title: chrono-Compatible Low-Level Date Algorithms
  type: article
  URL: https://howardhinnant.github.io/date_algorithms
...

```

As demonstrated by the previous sentence above and the [References](#references) section below, Quarto citations can include Dec dates. By default, Quarto formats citations according to the [Chicago Manual of Style](https://chicagomanualofstyle.org/) author-date format. Per this format, the citation for this article would appear as [Laptev, Martin](https://maptv.github.io/about). {{< meta date >}}. "Dec." \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://maptv.github.io/dec>.

Instead of using Quarto [citation syntax](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html#sec-citations), you can also prepare a list of references using [footnotes](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html#footnotes). As an example, I included citations for the Hinnant date algorithm article[^4] in both the [references](#references) and [footnotes](#footnotes) sections. In alphabetical order below, you will find a list of the [Observable](http://observablehq.com) notebooks that I adapted into many of the visualizations above.

# Appendix {.unnumbered #last}

# Observable notebooks {.unnumbered .appendix #note}

::: {.column-page-right #citelist}
00. [Armstrong, Zan](https://observablehq.com/@zanarmstrong) [2023+057]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 27, 2023"}. “Text color annotations in markdown.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/text-color-annotations-in-markdown>.
01. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock) [2020+335]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 30, 2021"}. “Time Zones.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@mbostock/time-zones>.
02. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock) [2022+037]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 7, 2022"}. “Solar Terminator.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@d3/solar-terminator>.
03. [Bostock, Mike](https://observablehq.com/@mbostock) [2023+314]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 9, 2024"}. “Input: Table.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/input-table>.
04. [Edwards, Paul](https://observablehq.com/@pjedwards). [2022+171]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="August 19, 2022"}. “Compass Rose as legend with colors.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@pjedwards/compass-rose-as-legend-with-colors>.
05. [Freedman, Dylan](https://observablehq.com/@freedmand). [2017+345]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="February 9, 2018"}. “Sounds.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@freedmand/sounds>.
06. [Gordon, Marcus A.](https://observablehq.com/@magfoto). [2018+288]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="December 14, 2018"}. “Wavelengths and Spectral Colours.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@magfoto/wavelengths-and-spectral-colours>.
07. [Harmath, Dénes](https://observablehq.com/user/@thsoft). [2018+104]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 13, 2018"}. “ABC.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@thsoft/abc>.
08. [Johnson, Ian](https://observablehq.com/@enjalot) [2021+121]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 30, 2021"}. “Draggable World Map Coordinates Input.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@enjalot/draggable-world-map-coordinates-input>.
09. [Lim, Maddie](https://observablehq.com/@maddievision) [2018+330]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 25, 2019"}. “Enneagram.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@maddievision/enneagram>.
10. [Paavanb](https://observablehq.com/@paavanb). [2024+006]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="March 7, 2024"}. “Progressive Color Picker.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@paavanb/progressive-color-picker>.
11. [Patel, Amit](https://observablehq.com/@redblobgames). [2021+290]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="December 16, 2021"}. “Compass Rose.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@paavanb/progressive-color-picker>.
12. [Pettiross, Jeff](https://observablehq.com/@pettiross) [2024+150]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="July 19, 2024"}. “Categorical color scheme test tool.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@observablehq/categorical-palette-tool>
13. [Rieder, Lukas](https://observablehq.com/@lukasrieder) [2023+032]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="April 2, 2023"}. “Editable table.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@parlant/editable-table>.
14. [Rivière, Philippe](https://observablehq.com/@fil) [2022+259]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="November 15, 2022"}. “Add a class to an observable input.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@recifs/add-a-class-to-an-observable-input--support>.
15. [Rivière, Philippe](https://observablehq.com/@fil) [2023+330]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 25, 2024"}. “D3 Projections.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@fil/d3-projections>.
16. [Yamahata, Christophe](https://observablehq.com/@christophe-yamahata) [2021+119]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 28, 2022"}. “Great circle: shortest distance between two locations on Earth 🌏.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://observablehq.com/@christophe-yamahata/great-circle-shortest-distance-between-two-locations-on-ea>.
:::

[^0]: [Newton, Issac](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isaac_Newton#:~:text=,an%20English%20polymath,-active%20as%20a). 1704. "Opticks." \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://doi.org/10.5479/sil.302475.39088000644674>.
[^1]: [Clint Goss](https://www.clintgoss.com). [2022+098]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="June 7, 2022"}. “Color of Sound.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://www.flutopedia.com/sound_color.htm>.
[^2]: [Collignon, Claude Boniface](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Boniface_Collignon#:~:text=a%20French%20attorney%20who%20contributed%20to%20scientific%20and%20social%20reforms%20in%20the%20time%20of%20the%20French%20Revolution). 1788. “Découverte d'étalons justes, naturels, invariables et universels.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://archive.org/details/dcouvertedtalon00collgoog/page/n68/mode/2up>.
[^3]: [Agnoli, Paolo](http://www.paoloagnoli.it) & [D'Agostini, Giulio](https://www.roma1.infn.it/~dagos). [2004+330]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="January 25, 2005"}. “Why does the meter beat the second?” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://arxiv.org/abs/physics/0412078>.
[^4]: [Hinnant, Howard](https://howardhinnant.github.io). [2021+185]{.tool data-bs-toggle="tooltip" data-bs-title="September 1, 2021"}. “`chrono`-Compatible Low-Level Date Algorithms.” \${decYearP0}+\${decDateP0}. <https://howardhinnant.github.io/date_algorithms.html>.

# Glossary {.unnumbered .appendix #glos}

::: {.column-page-right #gloslist}
- [a](#arcbeat){#ab}: arcbeat, a hundred thousandth of a circle, 0.0036 degrees, .216 arcminutes, 12.96 arcseconds
- [b](#beat){#b}: beat, centimilliday, a hundred thousandth of an day, 864 milliseconds
    - [mb](#millibeat){#mb}: millibeat, centimicroday, a thousandth of a beat, a hundred millionth of a day, 864 microseconds
- [bpc](#beatpercentiday){#bpc}: a musical or heart beat per centiday, a tenth of a beat per milliday, 0.069[4]{.vinculum} beats per minute, 100 beats per day
- [bpm](#beatpermilliday){#bpm}: a musical or heart beat per milliday, ten beats per centiday, 0.69[4]{.vinculum} beats per minute, 1000 beats per day
- [bmi](#bodymassindex){#bmi}: body mass index, kilograins of body mass divided by height in zem squared (kg/z&sup2;)
- [c](#taur){#c}: taur, 𝜏*r*, 100000 kilozem, 40000 kilometers, nearly the circumference of the Earth, roughly the product of 𝜏 and the radius of the Earth, approximately the dividend of the surface area and the diameter of the Earth
    - [mc](#millitaur){#mc}: millitaur, *m*𝜏*r*, a thousandth of a taur, 100 kilozem, 40 kilometers
    - [nc](#nanotaur){#nc}: nanotaur, *n*𝜏*r*, a thousandth of a taur, 100 millizem, 1 decizem, 4 centimeters
    - [nc&sup3;](#cubicnanotaur){#nc3}: cubic nanotaur, *n*𝜏*r*&sup3;, 1 cubic decizem
- [d](#day){#d}: day, a tenth of a decaday, a seventh of week, a fifth of a pentaday, 10 decidays, 24 hours, 100 centidays, 1000 millidays, 1440 minutes, 86400 seconds, 100000 beats, the inverse of a quotidie
    - [dek](#decaday){#dek}: decaday, a group of ten days, 2 pentadays
    - [pent](#pentaday){#pent}: pentaday, a group of five days, half a decaday
    - [dod](#dayofdek){#dod}: day of decaday
    - [dop](#dayofpent){#dop}: day of pentaday
    - [dom](#dayofmonth){#dom}: day of month
    - [dow](#dayofweek){#dow}: day of week
    - [doy](#dayofyear){#doy}: day of year, decaday * 10 + dod
    - [dd](#deciday){#dd}: deciday, a tenth of a day, 2.4 hours, 144 minutes
    - [cd](#centiday){#cd}: centiday, a hundredth of a day, 0.24 hours, 14.4 minutes
    - [md](#milliday){#md}: milliday, a thousandth of a day, 1.44 minutes
    - [cmd](#centimilliday){#cmd}: centimilliday, a hundred thousandth of a day, 1 beat, 864 milliseconds
    - [µd](#microday){#ud}: microday, a millionth of a day, 86.4 milliseconds
    - [nd](#nanoday){#nd}: nanoday, a billionth of a day, 86.4 microseconds
- [°](#degree){#deg}: degree, 1/360 turns, 180/𝜋 or 360/𝜏 radians
    - [c°](#compassdegree){#cdeg}: compass degree
    - [h°](#huedegree){#hdeg}: hue degree
- [e](#egg){#e}: egg, 1000 grains, 2 ounces, 64 grams
- [f](#foot){#f}: Dec foot, 0.75 zem, 75 millimeter 
    - [f&sup2;](#squarefoot){#f2}: Dec square foot, 5625 square centizem, 9 square decimeter
- [g](#gutta){#g}: drop (gutta in Latin) or grain (granum in Latin), 64 microliters or 64 milligrams
    - [kg](#kilograin){#kg}: kilograin or kilodrop, 64 grams or 64 milliliters
    - [Mg](#megagrain){#Mg}: megagrain or megadrop, 64 kilograms or 64 liters
- [h](#hand){#h}: hand, 0.25 zem, 1 decimeter
- [hex](#hexadecimal){#hex}: hexadecimal, base 16
- [hsl](#huesaturationlightness){#hsl}: hue saturation lightness
- [hsv](#huesaturationvalue){#hsv}: hue saturation value
- [i](#index){#i}: an index that represents either the ten musical steps in a musical octave that result in a two-fold increase in frequency or the ten days in a decaday; the Greek letter iota represents ten in the Greek numeral system
- [i](#inch){#i}: inch, a sixteenth of a zem, 25 millimeter
    - [i&sup2;](#squareinch){#i2}: Dec square inch, 3906.25 square millizem, 625 square millimeters
- [k](#keg){#k}: keg, cubic zem, 64 liters, 1000 wine glasses, a million drops, half a barrel
- [L](#liter){#L}: liter, 15625 drops, a cubic decimeter
    - [mL](#milliliter){#mL}: milliliter, a cubic centimeter, a thousandth of a liter, 15.625 drops
    - [µL](#microliter){#uL}: microliter, a cubic millimeter, a millionth of a liter, 0.015625 drops
- [m](#meridian){#m}: meridian, a full circle around the Earth moving North or South; used in the abbreviations a.m. (antemeridian) and p.m. (postmeridian)
    - [dm](#decimeridian){#dm}: decimeridian, a tenth of a meridian
    - [mm](#millimeridian){#mm}: millimeridian, a thousandth of a meridian
- [m&sup2;](#squaremeter){#m2}: square meter, 6.25 square zem
    - [km&sup2;](#squarekilometer){#km2}: square kilometer, 6.25 square kilozem
    - [dm&sup2;](#squaredecimeter){#dm2}: square decimeter, 6.25 square decizem
- [λ](#parallel){#lambda}: parallel, a full circle around the Earth moving West or East; the English alphabet equivalent of λ is the letter "l", which occurs three times in the word "parallel" and represents a ***l***ine that crosses every possible ***l***ongitude at the same ***l***atitude
    - [dλ](#deciparallel){#dlambda}: deciparallel, a tenth of a parallel
    - [mλ](#milliparallel){#mlambda}: milliparallel, a thousandth of a parallel
- [p](#perbeat){#per}: perbeat, the inverse of a beat, 1/beat, once per beat, every beat, 100000 q; the letter "p" can be flipped vertically to produce the letter "b"
    - [Tp](#teraperbeat){#Tp}: teraperbeat, 10^12^ perbeat, the inverse of a picobeat, 1/picobeat, once per picobeat, every picobeat
- [q](#quotidie){#q}: quotidie, the inverse of a day, a hundred thousandth of a perbeat; the letter "q" can be flipped vertically to produce the letter "d"
- [r](#rose){#r}: compass rose, a full circle along the horizon, 360 compass degress
    - [mr](#millirose){#mr}: compass millirose, a thousandth of a circle along the horizon, .36 compass degress
- [rad](#radian){#rad}: radian, $1\over\tau$ turns, $360\over\tau$ degrees, $1\over 2\pi$ turns, $180\over\pi$ degrees
- [rgb](#redgreenblue){#rgb}: red green blue
- [s](#step){#s}: step, a tenth of a two fold change in frequency from one octave to an adjacent octave
    - [Ds](#decastep){#Ds}: decastep, octave, a two fold change in frequency from one octave to an adjacent octave
    - [ms](#millistep){#ms}: millistep, a ten thousandth of a two fold change in frequency from one octave to an adjacent octave
- [SI](#internationalsystemofunits){#si}: [International System of Units](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_System_of_Units#:~:text=the%20world%27s%20most%20widely%20used%20system%20of%20measurement)
- [sol](#speedoflight){#sol}: speed of light, 647.55170928 kiloomegars, 299792458 meters per second
- [sos](#speedofsound){#sos}: speed of sound, 735.048 milliomegars, 340.3 meters per second
- [𝜏](#tau){#2pi}: 2𝜋 or approximately 6.2831853
- [Tenet](#tenequaltemperance){#tenet}: ten equal temperance
    - [Xet](#10et){#xet}: Tenet
    - [12et](#twelveequaltemperance){#12et}: twelve equal temperance
- [tod](#timeofday){#tod}: time of day
- [t](#turn){#t}: turn, 360 degrees, 𝜏 or 2𝜋 radians
    - [ct](#centiturn){#ct}: centiturn, a hundredth of a turn, 3.6 degrees, 𝜏/100 or 𝜋/50 radians
    - [dt](#deciturn){#dt}: deciturn, a tenth of a turn, 36 degrees, 𝜏/10 or 𝜋/5 radians
    - [mt](#milliturn){#mt}: milliturn, a thousandth of a turn, .36 degrees, 𝜏/1000 or 𝜋/500 r $\pi\over 500$ radians
- [tzo](#timezoneoffset){#tzo}: time zone offset
- [u](#ounce){#u}: ounce (uncia in Latin), 500 grains, 32 grams, 500 drops, 32 milliliters
- [utc](#coordinateduniversaltime){#utc}: [Coordinated Universal Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time#:~:text=the%20primary%20time%20standard%20globally%20used%20to%20regulate%20clocks%20and%20time)
- [US](#unitedstates){#us}: [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imperial_and_US_customary_measurement_systems)
- [v](#omegar){#v}: omegar, ωr, 1041.[6]{.vinculum} Dec miles per hour, approximately 1035.62 US miles per hour, 1.[6]{.vinculum} megameters per hour, 0.4[629]{.vinculum} kilometers per second, roughly 1.36 times the speed of sound
    - [kv](#kiloomegar){#kv}: kiloomegar, kωr, 1.[6]{.vinculum} gigameters per hour, 0.4[629]{.vinculum} megameters per second, approximately 0.1544% of the speed of light
    - [mv](#milliomegar){#mv}: milliomegar, mωr, 1.041[6]{.vinculum} Dec miles per hour, roughly 1.03562 US miles per hour, 1.[6]{.vinculum} kilometers per hour, 0.4[629]{.vinculum} meters per second, approximately 0.136% the speed of sound
- [w](#wineglass){#w}: wineglass, 64 milliters, 2 ounces, cubic decizem, 1000 drops
- [x](#hexamilliare){#x}: hexamilliare, square zem, z&sup2;, 16 square decimeters, 1.[7]{.vinculum} Dec square feet, 256 Dec square inches
    - [Mx](#megahexamilliare){#Mx}: megahexamilliare, a million square zem, square kilozem, kz&sup2;, hexakilare, 16 hectares, 1600 ares, 40 Dec acres, 0.16 square kilometers, 0.0625 Dec square miles
- [y](#year){#y}: year
    - [my](#milliyear){#my}: milliyear, a thousandth of a year
    - [yoe](#yearofera){#yoe}: year of era, integer years since the Dec epoch
- [z](#zoneequatorialmeter){#z}: zem, zone equatorial meter, 4 decimeters, 16 Dec inches
    - [kz&sup2;](#squarekilozem){#kz2}: square kilozem, a million square zem, megahexamilliare, Mx, hexakilare, 16 hectares, 1600 ares, 40 Dec acres, 0.16 square kilometers, 0.0625 Dec square miles
    - [kz](#kilozem){#kz}: kilozem, 1000 zem, 400 meters, a quarter Dec mile
    - [z&sup2;](#squarezem){#z2}: square zem, hexamilliare, 16 square decimeters, 1.[7]{.vinculum} Dec square feet, 256 Dec square inches
    - [Dz&sup2;](#squaredecazem){#Dz2}: square decazem, 1 hexadeciare, 16 square meters, 19.75 Dec square yards, 100 square zem
    - [z&sup3;](#cubiczem){#z3}: cubic zem, 1 keg, 64 liters, 1000 wine glasses, a million drops, half a barrel
    - [dz&sup3;](#cubicdecizem){#dz3}: cubic decizem, 1000 drops, 64 milliliters, 2 ounces, 1 wine glass
    - [cz&sup3;](#cubiccentizem){#cz3}: cubic centizem, 1 drop, 64 microliters
    - [dz](#decizem){#dz}: decizem, a tenth of a zem, 4 centimeters
    - [cz](#centizem){#cz}: centizem, a hundredth of a zem, 4 millimeters
    - [mz](#millizem){#mz}: millizem, a thousandth of a zem, 0.4 millimeters
:::

{{< include /dec/_index.qmd >}}
